title: What Is Sin?
subtitle: An inherited debt paid by Christ, or a free choice erased by repentance? Comparing how the Bible and the Quran define wrongdoing
summary: Christianity defines sin as 'lawlessness' (1 John 3:4) and, in mainstream teaching, as a condition every person inherits from Adam's fall, so serious that 'the wages of sin is death' (Romans 6:23) and only Christ's atonement can pay it. Islam teaches that every soul begins morally free and unstained — a claim the Quran grounds in the principle that 'no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another' (Quran 17:15) — and that sin is a choice ranging from minor lapses to grave major sins (kaba'ir), fully erased through sincere repentance offered directly to God (Quran 66:8). This article quotes each tradition's own scripture — Romans 3:23, Romans 6:23, 1 John 3:4, and 1 John 1:9 alongside Quran 17:15, 4:31, 53:32, 66:8, and 3:135 — before comparing how each understands what sin is and how it is removed.

seo.metaTitle: What Is Sin? Islam and Christianity Compared
seo.metaDescription: Is sin an inherited debt Christ must pay, or a free choice erased by repentance? Compare Romans 3:23 and 1 John 3:4 with the Quran's teaching on major sins.

sections.title: In One Minute
sections.body:
Christianity defines sin, in its simplest biblical statement, as lawlessness: 'Everyone who sins also commits lawlessness. Sin is lawlessness' (1 John 3:4). Mainstream Christian theology adds that every human being is born into this condition through Adam's fall (a doctrine explored fully in a companion article on original sin), so that 'all have sinned, and fall short of the glory of God' (Romans 3:23). Because 'the wages of sin is death' (Romans 6:23), sin functions like a debt that only the atoning death of Christ can pay; a believer's part is to confess, resting on that atonement, so that 'he is faithful and righteous to forgive us the sins' (1 John 1:9).

Islam starts from a different picture of the human being. Every soul is created morally free and unstained by any inherited guilt — the Quran states plainly that 'no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another' (Quran 17:15) — so sin (dhanb) is a choice a free person makes, not a condition imposed on a newborn. The Quran ranks wrongdoing from minor lapses to grave major sins, the kaba'ir (Quran 4:31, 53:32), and commands 'sincere repentance' (Quran 66:8) as the path back to God, describing the righteous as those who 'remember Allah and seek forgiveness for their sins' whenever they fall short (Quran 3:135). This article sets out both pictures in their own scriptures' words, then compares them.

sections.title: Why This Question Matters
sections.body:
Every religion answers, whether explicitly or not, a basic question: what exactly is wrong with human beings, and how deep does the problem go? The answer shapes everything downstream — what salvation means, what a savior would need to do, and what an ordinary believer does about their own wrongdoing on an average day.

The Bible's key word for sin, in the Greek New Testament, is hamartia, an archery term meaning to miss the mark. The apostle John narrows the definition further: sin is lawlessness, a violation of God's own standard (1 John 3:4). The Quran uses several words for wrongdoing — dhanb and ithm for sin in general, and kaba'ir for the gravest sins — and treats sin, in every case, as an act a responsible person chooses, not a condition inherited at birth, since, as the Quran states, 'no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another' (Quran 17:15).

Both traditions agree that human beings sin constantly and need God's forgiveness; neither offers a religion of moral self-sufficiency. Where they part ways is on three connected questions: does guilt begin at birth or at the first wrong choice; are all sins equal in weight; and what removes sin once it is committed. The rest of this article follows those questions through each tradition's own scriptures.

sections.title: The Question on the Table
sections.body:
Strip the topic to its center and one question does most of the work: is sin fundamentally a debt someone must pay, or a moral failure God forgives directly once a person sincerely turns back to Him?

Mainstream Christian theology answers that sin's penalty — death, both physical and spiritual — is too serious for repentance alone to settle. Romans 6:23 states the logic in a single sentence: 'the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.' Sin, on this view, is a debt recorded against every person, and it must be paid, not merely waived.

Islam answers differently. The Quran never presents forgiveness as requiring a substitute to bear a penalty. Sin is removed when a person turns to God in genuine repentance — 'repent to Allah with sincere repentance' (Quran 66:8) — and God, described as vast in mercy, forgives directly. A second question follows close behind the first: are all sins equally serious? The Quran's own language distinguishes 'major sins' from lesser ones (Quran 4:31, 53:32), a distinction the sections below explore alongside a similar debate within Christianity itself.

sections.title: How Each Tradition Worked Out Its Answer
sections.body:
Christian reflection on exactly why Christ's death deals with sin developed gradually over centuries and was never settled by a single council the way the doctrine of the Trinity was. The medieval theologian Anselm of Canterbury, writing in the eleventh century, proposed what became known as satisfaction theory in his work Cur Deus Homo ('Why God Became Man'): human sin dishonors God by an infinite offense, so only someone both fully human and fully divine could offer satisfaction adequate to restore that honor. Several centuries later, the Protestant Reformers Martin Luther and John Calvin, in the sixteenth century, developed penal substitution, a related but distinct account in which Christ does not merely restore God's honor but bears the actual legal penalty — death — that sinners owed. Both theories remain influential in different Christian traditions today, and both assume the same starting point: that sin's penalty must be paid by someone.

Islamic scholarship, working from the Quran and the practice of the Prophet Muhammad, developed its own literature cataloguing the varieties of sin. The best-known single work is Al-Kaba'ir ('The Major Sins'), written by the Damascus-based scholar Shams al-Din al-Dhahabi (1274–1348), which draws on Quranic verses and prophetic teaching to catalogue roughly seventy sins classed as major. That project only makes sense against the Quran's own explicit two-tier language of major and minor sins, quoted in full below, and its confidence that sincere repentance — not a payment made by someone else — is what removes them.

sections.title: What the Quran Says
sections.body:
The Quran's vocabulary for wrongdoing assumes, from the outset, a morally free agent who chooses to sin and remains capable of choosing to stop. Surah Al-Israa states the underlying principle of purely personal accountability on which that assumption rests:

مَّنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًۭا

'Whoever is guided is only guided for [the benefit of] his soul. And whoever errs only errs against it. And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And never would We punish until We sent a messenger.' (Quran 17:15)

If, as this verse states, no soul bears the burden of another, then a child cannot inherit Adam's guilt for a sin only Adam committed; guilt, on this principle, is strictly personal, attached to the one who acts rather than passed down a bloodline. That is the textual basis for a claim this article makes repeatedly: the Quran nowhere describes a newborn as guilty, because sin enters the story only once a responsible person acts.

The Quran also grades sin rather than treating every lapse as equally catastrophic. Surah An-Nisaa promises that avoiding the gravest wrongs brings a remarkable mercy for the smaller ones:

إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا۟ كَبَآئِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَنُدْخِلْكُم مُّدْخَلًۭا كَرِيمًۭا

'If you avoid the major sins which you are forbidden, We will remove from you your lesser sins and admit you to a noble entrance [into Paradise].' (Quran 4:31)

Surah An-Najm draws the same two-tier picture and adds an important warning against self-righteousness:

ٱلَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبَٰٓئِرَ ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ إِلَّا ٱللَّمَمَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ وَٰسِعُ ٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ ۚ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ إِذْ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِذْ أَنتُمْ أَجِنَّةٌۭ فِى بُطُونِ أُمَّهَٰتِكُمْ ۖ فَلَا تُزَكُّوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۖ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰٓ

'Those who avoid the major sins and immoralities, only [committing] slight ones. Indeed, your Lord is vast in forgiveness. He was most knowing of you when He produced you from the earth and when you were fetuses in the wombs of your mothers. So do not claim yourselves to be pure; He is most knowing of who fears Him.' (Quran 53:32)

That last clause matters: even a description of 'those who avoid the major sins' ends with a warning against moral self-congratulation. No one, in the Quran's view, has earned the right to call themselves pure.

How, then, is sin removed? Not through a substitute bearing a penalty, but through repentance addressed directly to God. Surah At-Tahrim commands it in the plainest terms:

يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوْبَةًۭ نَّصُوحًا عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يُكَفِّرَ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَيُدْخِلَكُمْ جَنَّٰتٍۢ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَٰرُ يَوْمَ لَا يُخْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّبِىَّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ ۖ نُورُهُمْ يَسْعَىٰ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمَٰنِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ أَتْمِمْ لَنَا نُورَنَا وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍۢ قَدِيرٌۭ

'O you who have believed, repent to Allah with sincere repentance. Perhaps your Lord will remove from you your misdeeds and admit you into gardens beneath which rivers flow [on] the Day when Allah will not disgrace the Prophet and those who believed with him. Their light will proceed before them and on their right; they will say, "Our Lord, perfect for us our light and forgive us. Indeed, You are over all things competent."' (Quran 66:8)

And Surah Aal-i-Imraan describes what that sincere repentance looks like in the life of an ordinary believer, and names who alone can grant it:

وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا فَعَلُوا۟ فَٰحِشَةً أَوْ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ ذَكَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَٱسْتَغْفَرُوا۟ لِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَمَن يَغْفِرُ ٱلذُّنُوبَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ وَلَمْ يُصِرُّوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلُوا۟ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ

'And those who, when they commit an immorality or wrong themselves [by transgression], remember Allah and seek forgiveness for their sins - and who can forgive sins except Allah? - and [who] do not persist in what they have done while they know.' (Quran 3:135)

Put together, these five verses give sin, in Islam, a clear shape: guilt that is strictly personal and never inherited (Quran 17:15); a graded scale from minor to major; a God whose forgiveness is described as vast; a remedy — sincere repentance, addressed directly to Him — available the moment a person recognizes their fault; and a firm rejection of persisting in wrongdoing, or of considering oneself already pure.

sections.title: What the Bible Says
sections.body:
The Bible's short definition of sin comes from the apostle John:

'Everyone who sins also commits lawlessness. Sin is lawlessness.' (1 John 3:4)

On this definition, sin is not primarily a feeling or a mistake but a violation of God's law — a legal category as much as a moral one. Paul's letter to the Romans then makes the scope of the problem universal, applying to every human being without exception:

'for all have sinned, and fall short of the glory of God;' (Romans 3:23)

Mainstream Christian theology reads this verse alongside Romans 5, where Paul traces universal sinfulness back to Adam's disobedience in the Garden (a connection explored in full in this library's article on original sin and personal responsibility). Whatever its origin, the consequence Paul names next is severe:

'For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.' (Romans 6:23)

The word 'wages' casts sin as a transaction: it is earned, like pay for labor, and what it earns is death. Historic Christian doctrine reads this as more than physical death — it is spiritual separation from God, the debt Christ's crucifixion is understood to settle on the sinner's behalf. That is why the verse does not end with the wage; it ends by contrasting it with a 'free gift,' received rather than earned, available 'in Christ Jesus.'

For someone already trusting in that atonement, the ongoing Christian remedy for sins committed after coming to faith is confession, not a fresh sacrifice:

'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and righteous to forgive us the sins, and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.' (1 John 1:9)

Notice what grounds the forgiveness here: God's own faithfulness and righteousness, not the size or sincerity of the confession by itself. In the classic Protestant and Catholic reading alike, this verse describes a believer's ongoing relationship with a God who has already dealt with sin's ultimate penalty through Christ; confession restores fellowship rather than re-earning a salvation already secured. Taken together, these four verses present sin as lawlessness (1 John 3:4), as a universal condition (Romans 3:23), as a debt whose wage is death (Romans 6:23), and as something dealt with through Christ's atonement, to which the believer responds with ongoing confession (1 John 1:9).

sections.title: Reading the Texts Side by Side
sections.body:
Both traditions agree on more than casual observers might expect. Each insists sin is universal — 'all have sinned' (Romans 3:23) matches the Quran's assumption that every human being needs repentance (Quran 3:135, 66:8). Each insists sin is serious, not a minor slip to shrug off. And each insists forgiveness is real and available, not merely hoped for.

The traditions diverge on mechanism. Romans 6:23 frames sin as a debt earned by 'wages' and settled through a 'free gift' secured 'in Christ Jesus' — forgiveness routed through a substitutionary atonement. The Quran, across all four verses quoted above, never introduces a third party bearing sin's penalty; it repeats instead that God 'forgives sins' directly (Quran 3:135) to those who 'repent to Allah with sincere repentance' (Quran 66:8) and who do not persist in wrongdoing once they know better (Quran 3:135). Where Christianity's remedy runs through the cross, Islam's remedy runs through the sincere turning of the heart.

The traditions also converge, more than is often noticed, on grading sin. The Quran separates 'major sins' from lesser ones twice (Quran 4:31, 53:32). Catholic theology likewise distinguishes mortal sins, which sever a person's relationship with God, from venial sins, which wound but do not sever it — a structure with real resemblance to kaba'ir and lesser sins. Orthodox Christianity recognizes a comparable difference in gravity between sins, though it frames that difference somewhat differently and has not formalized an identical mortal/venial category system. Many Protestant traditions instead read 1 John 3:4's flat definition — 'sin is lawlessness' — as leveling all sin before a holy God. So whether sin comes in degrees divides opinion within Christianity nearly as much as it divides Christianity from Islam.

sections.title: Common Misunderstandings on Both Sides
sections.body:
Several frequent misreadings deserve correction on both sides.

First, Islam's teaching that sin is forgiven through repentance is sometimes mistaken for a claim that forgiveness is cheap or automatic. The Quran's own language resists that reading: repentance must be 'sincere' (Quran 66:8), and the person repenting must genuinely stop — 'do not persist in what they have done while they know' (Quran 3:135). Classical scholarship on repentance requires remorse, an actual halt to the wrongdoing, and a firm resolve not to return to it.

Second, Islam's teaching that no soul is born guilty is sometimes mistaken for a claim that Islam takes sin lightly once a person is accountable. The Quran's own warning — 'do not claim yourselves to be pure' (Quran 53:32) — guards precisely against that misreading.

Third, the Christian teaching that confession secures forgiveness (1 John 1:9) is sometimes flattened into 'say sorry and it is fine,' detached from the atonement it presupposes. In mainstream Christian theology, this verse addresses people already resting on Christ's finished work; it describes restored fellowship, not a stand-alone transaction.

Fourth, grading sins into major and minor categories is not uniquely Islamic. Catholic theology has long distinguished mortal from venial sin; Orthodox Christianity likewise treats some sins as graver than others, though it frames that distinction somewhat differently and does not share an identical, formalized mortal/venial category system. Even where the traditions converge on grading sin, the underlying theology of atonement differs sharply from Islam's theology of direct repentance.

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
Drawing the Quran's verses together, Islam's picture of sin holds together as one coherent claim about God's justice and mercy. Human beings are created free and morally unstained — no soul, the Quran insists, 'will bear the burden of another' (Quran 17:15) — so sin is something a person does, not a debt attached at birth. Sin is real and comes in degrees — the Quran names 'major sins' distinctly from lesser lapses (Quran 4:31, 53:32) — but no degree of sin outruns God's mercy, described as 'vast' (Quran 53:32). The remedy God has appointed is not a payment exacted from someone else; it is 'sincere repentance' offered directly to Him (Quran 66:8), available the instant a person remembers God, seeks forgiveness, and stops (Quran 3:135).

Muslims find this picture both just and merciful: just, because no one inherits another's guilt or needs another to die for their own choices; merciful, because forgiveness is never further away than a sincere turning of the heart, and God Himself poses the question that closes the case — 'who can forgive sins except Allah?' (Quran 3:135). The invitation of this article is to weigh that picture against the biblical texts quoted here — 'sin is lawlessness' (1 John 3:4), 'all have sinned' (Romans 3:23), 'the wages of sin is death' (Romans 6:23), 'confess our sins' (1 John 1:9) — and ask which account of a just, merciful God, and of the sinner standing before Him, sits most naturally together.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Does Islam teach that humans are born sinful?
A: No. The Quran states that 'no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another' (Quran 17:15), so every soul is treated as morally free and unstained at birth; sin only enters the picture once a person makes a wrong choice as a responsible agent. This is explored further in the companion article on original sin and personal responsibility.

Q: Are all sins equally serious in Islam?
A: No. The Quran distinguishes 'major sins' (kaba'ir) from lesser, 'slight' ones (Quran 4:31, 53:32), and promises that avoiding the major sins brings mercy for the smaller ones. Classical scholars, most famously al-Dhahabi in his work Al-Kaba'ir, catalogued and discussed these major sins in detail.

Q: Does Christianity teach that all sins carry the same weight?
A: Christians disagree among themselves. Catholic and Orthodox theology distinguish mortal sins from venial sins, a grading not unlike the Quran's major and minor sins. Many Protestant traditions instead read verses like 1 John 3:4 as treating all sin as lawlessness before a holy God, even while granting that sins differ in their earthly consequences.

Q: Why does Christianity say sin requires Christ's death, while Islam says it is removed by repentance?
A: The two traditions picture sin's mechanism differently. Romans 6:23 describes sin's penalty as a debt ('wages') paid through Christ's atoning death. The Quran never introduces a substitute bearing that penalty; it presents God as forgiving directly in response to 'sincere repentance' (Quran 66:8), because He is 'vast in forgiveness' (Quran 53:32).

Q: Can a Muslim ever be certain their sins are forgiven?
A: The Quran promises that God's forgiveness is 'vast' (Quran 53:32) and open to whoever turns to Him with sincere repentance and does not return to the wrong once they know better (Quran 3:135). Muslims are taught to combine hope in that mercy with continued watchfulness over their own conduct, rather than either despair or presumption.

sections.title: Sources and Further Reading
sections.body:
Every Quran verse quoted in this article was retrieved from api.alquran.cloud (Tanzil Uthmani Arabic text with the Saheeh International translation), and every Bible verse from bible-api.com (World English Bible, a public-domain translation); each appears here exactly as returned. The historical claims about Anselm of Canterbury's satisfaction theory and the Reformers' penal substitution theory were checked against the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy's entry on atonement; the dates and description of al-Dhahabi's Al-Kaba'ir were checked against standard bibliographic sources for that work.

Readers who want to go deeper can consult the works below: classic statements of Christian atonement theology, a standard survey of Christian doctrine, the best-known Islamic catalogue of major sins, and accessible introductions to repentance in Islamic practice.

quranVerses.arabic: مَّنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًۭا
quranVerses.translation: Whoever is guided is only guided for [the benefit of] his soul. And whoever errs only errs against it. And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And never would We punish until We sent a messenger.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا۟ كَبَآئِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَنُدْخِلْكُم مُّدْخَلًۭا كَرِيمًۭا
quranVerses.translation: If you avoid the major sins which you are forbidden, We will remove from you your lesser sins and admit you to a noble entrance [into Paradise].

quranVerses.translation: Those who avoid the major sins and immoralities, only [committing] slight ones. Indeed, your Lord is vast in forgiveness. He was most knowing of you when He produced you from the earth and when you were fetuses in the wombs of your mothers. So do not claim yourselves to be pure; He is most knowing of who fears Him.

quranVerses.arabic: يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوْبَةًۭ نَّصُوحًا عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يُكَفِّرَ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَيُدْخِلَكُمْ جَنَّٰتٍۢ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَٰرُ يَوْمَ لَا يُخْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّبِىَّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ ۖ نُورُهُمْ يَسْعَىٰ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمَٰنِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ أَتْمِمْ لَنَا نُورَنَا وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍۢ قَدِيرٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: O you who have believed, repent to Allah with sincere repentance. Perhaps your Lord will remove from you your misdeeds and admit you into gardens beneath which rivers flow [on] the Day when Allah will not disgrace the Prophet and those who believed with him. Their light will proceed before them and on their right; they will say, "Our Lord, perfect for us our light and forgive us. Indeed, You are over all things competent."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا فَعَلُوا۟ فَٰحِشَةً أَوْ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ ذَكَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَٱسْتَغْفَرُوا۟ لِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَمَن يَغْفِرُ ٱلذُّنُوبَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ وَلَمْ يُصِرُّوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلُوا۟ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ
quranVerses.translation: And those who, when they commit an immorality or wrong themselves [by transgression], remember Allah and seek forgiveness for their sins - and who can forgive sins except Allah? - and [who] do not persist in what they have done while they know.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: for all have sinned, and fall short of the glory of God;
For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.
Everyone who sins also commits lawlessness. Sin is lawlessness.
If we confess our sins, he is faithful and righteous to forgive us the sins, and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.

faq.question: Does Islam teach that humans are born sinful?
faq.answer: No. The Quran states that 'no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another' (Quran 17:15), so every soul is treated as morally free and unstained at birth; sin only enters the picture once a person makes a wrong choice as a responsible agent. This is explored further in the companion article on original sin and personal responsibility.

faq.question: Are all sins equally serious in Islam?
faq.answer: No. The Quran distinguishes 'major sins' (kaba'ir) from lesser, 'slight' ones (Quran 4:31, 53:32), and promises that avoiding the major sins brings mercy for the smaller ones. Classical scholars, most famously al-Dhahabi in his work Al-Kaba'ir, catalogued and discussed these major sins in detail.

faq.question: Does Christianity teach that all sins carry the same weight?
faq.answer: Christians disagree among themselves. Catholic and Orthodox theology distinguish mortal sins from venial sins, a grading not unlike the Quran's major and minor sins. Many Protestant traditions instead read verses like 1 John 3:4 as treating all sin as lawlessness before a holy God, even while granting that sins differ in their earthly consequences.

faq.question: Why does Christianity say sin requires Christ's death, while Islam says it is removed by repentance?
faq.answer: The two traditions picture sin's mechanism differently. Romans 6:23 describes sin's penalty as a debt ('wages') paid through Christ's atoning death. The Quran never introduces a substitute bearing that penalty; it presents God as forgiving directly in response to 'sincere repentance' (Quran 66:8), because He is 'vast in forgiveness' (Quran 53:32).

faq.question: Can a Muslim ever be certain their sins are forgiven?
faq.answer: The Quran promises that God's forgiveness is 'vast' (Quran 53:32) and open to whoever turns to Him with sincere repentance and does not return to the wrong once they know better (Quran 3:135). Muslims are taught to combine hope in that mercy with continued watchfulness over their own conduct, rather than either despair or presumption.

furtherReading.title: Cur Deus Homo (Why God Became Man)
furtherReading.author: Anselm of Canterbury
furtherReading.note: The medieval work that established the satisfaction theory of atonement, the classical Christian answer to why Christ's death was necessary for sin.

furtherReading.title: Institutes of the Christian Religion
furtherReading.author: John Calvin
furtherReading.note: A foundational Reformation text in which the penal substitution understanding of Christ's atoning death is developed at length.

furtherReading.title: Christian Theology: An Introduction
furtherReading.author: Alister E. McGrath
furtherReading.note: A widely used academic textbook surveying Christian doctrines of sin and atonement across traditions.

furtherReading.title: Al-Kaba'ir (The Major Sins)
furtherReading.author: Shams al-Din al-Dhahabi
furtherReading.note: The classical Islamic catalogue of major sins, drawing on Quranic verses and prophetic teaching.

furtherReading.title: Riyad as-Salihin
furtherReading.author: Imam al-Nawawi
furtherReading.note: A widely used classical compilation including chapters on repentance (tawbah) and seeking God's forgiveness.

furtherReading.title: Islam in Focus
furtherReading.author: Hammudah Abdalati
furtherReading.note: An accessible introduction to Islamic belief and practice, including sin, repentance, and human nature.
